Kaiming He initialization for m in self.modules(): if isinstance(m, nn.Linear): nn.init.kaiming_normal_(m.weight, nonlinearity='relu') nn.init.zeros_(m.bias) def forward(self, x): x = self.flatten(x) x = F.relu(self.fc1(x)) x = self.dropout(x) x = F.relu(self.fc2(x)) x = self.dropout(x) x = F.relu(self.fc3(x)) x = self.dropout(x) x = self.fc4(x) return x ``` **Mark split (a):** Model class with 3 hidden layers (2) + Kaiming init (2) + Forward with ReLU+Dropout (2) + Softmax via CrossEntropy (1) = **7 marks** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Training loop (snippet): ```python import torchvision, torchvision.transforms as T import torch.optim as optim transform = T.Compose([T.ToTensor(), T.Normalize((0.4914,0.4822,0.4465), (0.2470,0.2435,0.2616))]) train_set = torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10('./data', train=True, download=True, transform=transform) train_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(train_set, batch_size=128, shuffle=True) def train(model, weight_decay=0.0, use_dropout=False, epochs=10): model.to(device) opt = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=weight_decay) crit = nn.CrossEntropyLoss() losses = [] for ep in range(epochs): model.train() total = 0 for x, y in train_loader: x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device) opt.zero_grad() out = model(x) loss = crit(out, y) loss.backward() opt.step() total += loss.item() losses.append(total/len(train_loader)) return losses ``` **Comparison table:** | Config | Train Loss ↓ | Test Acc ↑ | Overfit Gap | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Kaiming+Adam | Lowest | ~53% | Largest | | (ii) + Dropout | Slightly higher | ~56% | Moderate | | (iii) + Drop+L2 | Stable | **~58–60%** | **Smallest** | **Conclusion (comment for full marks):** Configuration (iii) generalizes best because **dropout prevents co-adaptation** of neurons, while **L2 penalizes large weight magnitudes**, jointly reducing overfitting. **BatchNorm** further stabilizes training. (1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks for the comparison/comment, rest for code & table.) --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B. [KTU University Exam - June 2025 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain **Xavier (Glorot)** and **Kaiming (He)** initialization with their mathematical formulations. State clearly when each should be used and **why Kaiming is preferred for ReLU networks**. (7 marks) **(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **forward and backward pass** of a 3-hidden-layer feed-forward network for CIFAR-10 classification. Write the mathematical expression for cross-entropy loss with L2 regularization and explain the role of weight decay. (7 marks) ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Xavier (Glorot) Initialization** (Glorot & Bengio, 2010): $$W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[-\sqrt{\frac{6}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}},\; \sqrt{\frac{6}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}}\right]$$ or equivalently in Normal form $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}\right)$. **Derived under assumption:** activation is symmetric (sigmoid/tanh) and $n_{\text{in}} \approx n_{\text{out}}$. It maintains both forward and backward variance stability by averaging the two constraints $1/n_{\text{in}}$ and $1/n_{\text{out}}$. **Kaiming (He) Initialization** (He et al., 2015): $$W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}\right)$$ **Derived for ReLU**, where activations are zeroed for half the inputs on average. This requires a **factor of 2** (compared to Xavier) to keep the variance of pre-activations constant across layers. **Why Kaiming is preferred for ReLU:** ReLU activation is asymmetric: $a = \max(0, z)$ zeroes out negative values. With Xavier, the variance of activations would **halve** at every layer, leading to vanishing activations in deep networks. Kaiming's $\frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ compensates exactly for this halving, keeping $\text{Var}(a^{(l)}) \approx \text{Var}(a^{(l-1)})$. (2 marks for formulas + 2 marks for derivation + 2 marks for ReLU reasoning + 1 mark for usage scenario = 7 marks) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Forward Pass** (see diagram in Section 4.3): $$a^{(0)} = x \in \mathbb{R}^{3072}$$ $$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} a^{(0)} + b^{(1)}, \quad a^{(1)} = \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)})$$ $$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}, \quad a^{(2)} = \text{ReLU}(z^{(2)})$$ $$z^{(3)} = W^{(3)} a^{(2)} + b^{(3)}, \quad a^{(3)} = \text{ReLU}(z^{(3)})$$ $$z^{(4)} = W^{(4)} a^{(3)} + b^{(4)}, \quad \hat{y} = \text{Softmax}(z^{(4)})$$ **Cross-Entropy Loss with L2 Regularization:** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{10} y_{ic} \log(\hat{y}_{ic}) + \lambda \sum_{l=1}^{4} \Vert W^{(l)} \Vert_2^2$$ **Role of Weight Decay (L2):** The penalty $\lambda \sum \Vert W \Vert^2$ discourages large weights, leading to a **smoother decision boundary** that generalizes better on unseen data. In PyTorch, `weight_decay` in optimizers implements this directly through gradient update $W \leftarrow W - \eta(\nabla \mathcal{L} + 2\lambda W)$. (2 marks for forward pass equations + 2 marks for loss expression + 2 marks for backward explanation + 1 mark for weight decay role = 7 marks) --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Xavier with Kaiming**: Many students write Xavier for ReLU networks. Kaiming is the **correct** choice for ReLU (5–7 mark questions specifically test this). > 2. **Forgetting Softmax in output**: `nn.CrossEntropyLoss` already applies LogSoftmax internally — adding another `Softmax` layer is a **bug**, not a feature. > 3. **Wrong input flattening**: CIFAR-10 images are $32\times32\times3 = 3072$ dim. Forgetting `nn.Flatten()` causes dimension mismatch errors. > 4. **Not setting `model.eval()` during testing**: This **disables dropout** and uses BN running statistics. Forgetting it gives wrong accuracy. > 5. **Mixing up L1 and L2**: L1 = sum of **absolute** values (sparse), L2 = sum of **squared** values (smooth). > 6. **Using `weight_decay` in Adam without reason**: It works, but be ready to explain that Adam's adaptive moments make weight decay interact with momentum — modern practice is **AdamW** (decoupled weight decay). > 7. **Plotting without labels/legend**: KTU lab evaluation strictly deducts marks for unlabeled axes in graphs. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU Exam:** - **3-Hidden-Layer FFNN Architecture**: $3072 \rightarrow 1024 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 10$ (Input → H1 → H2 → H3 → Output) - **CIFAR-10 Specs**: $32\times32\times3$, 10 classes, 60000 images, normalize with mean $(0.4914, 0.4822, 0.4465)$ and std $(0.2470, 0.2435, 0.2616)$ - **Xavier Formula** (sigmoid/tanh): $\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}$ - **Kaiming Formula** (ReLU): $\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ - **Cross-Entropy Loss**: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ - **Softmax**: $\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ - **L1 Penalty**: $\lambda \sum \vert W \vert$ → **sparse** weights - **L2 Penalty**: $\lambda \sum W^2$ → **small** weights - **Dropout**: Random zero-out with probability $p$; **inverted dropout** scales by $\frac{1}{1-p}$ during training only - **BatchNorm**: $\hat{z} = \frac{z - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}}$ then scale-shift; allows higher learning rate - **SGD with Momentum**: $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla\mathcal{L}$ then $W \leftarrow W - \eta v_t$ - **Adam**: Combines momentum ($m_t$) + RMSProp ($v_t$) with bias correction - **Inference Mode**: `model.eval()` disables dropout, uses BN running stats - **Common Pitfall**: Apply `nn.Flatten()` before first Linear layer for image data - **Hyperparameter Defaults**: batch_size=128, lr=1e-3 (Adam), lr=0.01 (SGD), dropout_p=0.3 - **Expected Result Hierarchy**: Kaiming+Adam+Dropout+L2+BN **>** Kaiming+Adam **>** Xavier+SGD **>** Random+SGD

topics.title: Digit classification using CNN architecture for MNIST dataset and identify the performance change through pre-trained networks such as VGGNet or GoogleNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADL607 - DEEP LEARNING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Digit classification using CNN architecture for MNIST dataset and identify the performance change through pre-trained networks such as VGGNet or GoogleNet # 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **MNIST Digit Classification** is the canonical benchmark problem in computer vision where a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) must learn to map 28×28 grayscale handwritten digit images (from the Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology database) to one of 10 discrete class labels (0–9). The experiment extends this baseline to a **comparative transfer-learning analysis** using deep pre-trained architectures such as **VGGNet (VGG-16)** and **GoogLeNet (Inception-v1)** to quantify the performance delta achieved by leveraging hierarchical feature representations learned from large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *This experiment satisfies Course Outcome **CO2**: "Implement deep learning models for image classification using CNN architectures and evaluate performance improvements through transfer learning with pre-trained networks."* ## 1.2 Dataset Specification — MNIST | Property | Value | |---|---| | Total Images | **70,000** (60,000 train + 10,000 test) | | Image Resolution | **28 × 28 × 1** (grayscale) | | Number of Classes | **10** (digits 0–9) | | Pixel Intensity Range | **[0, 255]** → normalized to **[0, 1]** | | Storage Format | IDX file format / PyTorch `torchvision.datasets.MNIST` | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stamp Inspector" 🖋️ Imagine a post-office stamp inspector who has been trained to recognize digits. Initially, they only know the **shape outlines** (edges). With experience, they learn **loops, curves, intersections** (textures), then **stroke patterns** (parts of digits), and finally the **complete digit identity**. A **basic CNN** acts like the novice inspector learning directly from MNIST stamps. A **pre-trained VGGNet/GoogLeNet**, however, has *already* spent years studying millions of images of cats, cars, and faces — when it sees an MNIST digit, it brings an enormous visual vocabulary to the table. We merely **fine-tune** the last layer to teach it "this small 28×28 pattern is a '7'." This is the essence of **Transfer Learning**. ## 1.4 Why Pre-trained Networks for MNIST? > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Although MNIST is "easy," using VGGNet/GoogLeNet demonstrates the **engineering principle of transfer learning** — a critical KTU evaluation point. The MNIST test here is a *pedagogical vehicle* to measure feature-transferability, not to chase the highest accuracy. > [!WARNING] > **Architectural Mismatch Warning:** VGGNet and GoogLeNet expect **3-channel (RGB) input of size 224×224**. MNIST images (28×28, 1-channel) **must be upsampled and replicated** across channels before being fed into these networks. ## 1.5 Pre-trained Networks — Snapshot - **VGG-16**: 16 weight layers (13 conv + 3 FC), uniform 3×3 convolutions, ~138M parameters. Known for **simplicity and depth**. - **GoogLeNet (Inception-v1)**: 22 layers deep, uses **Inception modules** (parallel 1×1, 3×3, 5×5 convs), ~6.8M parameters. Known for **computational efficiency via parameter reduction**. --- # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The CNN Forward Pass A CNN processes an input image through a hierarchical feature extraction pipeline: ### Stage 1: Convolution Operation The convolution layer slides a learnable filter (kernel) $\mathbf{K}$ of size $k \times k$ across the input $\mathbf{I}$ to produce a feature map $\mathbf{S}$: $$S_{i,j} = (\mathbf{I} * \mathbf{K})_{i,j} = \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} I_{i+m,\, j+n} \cdot K_{m,n} + b$$ where $b$ is the **bias term**. ### Stage 2: ReLU Activation (Non-linearity) To introduce non-linearity (without which a deep CNN collapses to a single linear layer): $$f(x) = \max(0,\, x)$$ ### Stage 3: Pooling (Spatial Down-sampling) **Max Pooling** (most common) downsamples feature maps: $$P_{i,j} = \max_{(m,n) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} S_{m,n}$$ where $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}$ is the pooling window (typically 2×2 with stride 2). ### Stage 4: Fully Connected + Softmax Classification The flattened feature vector is fed to a softmax layer producing class probabilities: $$P(y=c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}, \quad C = 10$$ ### Stage 5: Loss Computation — Categorical Cross-Entropy $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$ where $y_{i,c}$ is the true one-hot label and $\hat{y}_{i,c}$ is the predicted probability. ## 2.2 Output Dimension Formula (KTU High-Yield) For any convolutional or pooling layer: $$W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{\text{in}} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ where: - $W_{\text{in}}$ = input dimension - $p$ = padding - $k$ = kernel size - $s$ = stride > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic:** "*In plus two-P minus K, divide by S, add one, take the floor.*" Examiners love testing this! ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Convolution Output Size | $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(W_{\text{in}} + 2p - k)/s\rfloor + 1$ | $W_{\text{in}}, p, k, s$ | CNN architecture design | | 2 | ReLU Activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | Non-linearity injection | | 3 | Softmax | $P_c = e^{z_c}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ | $z_c$ = logit | Multi-class probability | | 4 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | $y_c, \hat{y}_c$ | Training objective | | 5 | Max Pooling | $P_{i,j} = \max_{(m,n) \in \mathcal{R}} S_{m,n}$ | $S$ = feature map | Spatial reduction | | 6 | Receptive Field | $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin every modern vision system — from **autonomous vehicles** (real-time object detection) to **medical imaging** (tumor classification) to **OCR systems** (license plate recognition). The MNIST experiment is a *microcosm* of these industrial pipelines. ## 2.4 Transfer Learning Strategy Two paradigms: **1. Feature Extraction (Frozen Backbone)** - Freeze all convolutional layers of VGG/GoogLeNet - Replace final FC layer with a 10-class softmax - Train *only* the new classifier head **2. Fine-Tuning** - Unfreeze the last few convolutional blocks - Train with a small learning rate (e.g., $10^{-4}$) - Allows the network to adapt high-level features to MNIST --- # 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Dimension Walk-Through — Basic CNN for MNIST Consider this canonical PyTorch architecture (the *de facto* KTU lab model): ```text Input (1 × 28 × 28) → Conv2d(1, 32, kernel=3, padding=1) → ReLU → Conv2d(32, 64, kernel=3, padding=1) → ReLU → MaxPool2d(2, 2) → Conv2d(64, 128, kernel=3, padding=1) → ReLU → MaxPool2d(2, 2) → Flatten → Linear(128 × 7 × 7, 256) → ReLU → Dropout(0.5) → Linear(256, 10) → Softmax ``` ### Step-by-Step Dimensional Derivation **Step 1 — First Conv Block (Input 1×28×28)** Using $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 2(1) - 3)/1\rfloor + 1$: $$ W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 2 - 3)/1\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 27 \rfloor + 1 = 28 $$ Output volume: **32 × 28 × 28** (32 channels, spatial 28×28 preserved by padding=1). **Step 2 — Second Conv Block (32→64 channels, 28×28)** $$ W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 2 - 3)/1\rfloor + 1 = 28 $$ Output volume: **64 × 28 × 28**. **Step 3 — First MaxPool (2×2, stride 2)** $$ W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 0 - 2)/2\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 13 \rfloor + 1 = 14 $$ Output volume: **64 × 14 × 14**. **Step 4 — Third Conv Block (64→128 channels, 14×14)** $$ W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(14 + 2 - 3)/1\rfloor + 1 = 14 $$ Output volume: **128 × 14 × 14**. **Step 5 — Second MaxPool (2×2, stride 2)** $$ W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(14 + 0 - 2)/2\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 6 \rfloor + 1 = 7 $$ Output volume: **128 × 7 × 7**. **Step 6 — Flatten** Flattened vector size: $128 \times 7 \times 7 = 6{,}272$. **Step 7 — FC1 (6,272 → 256)** Output: **256**-dimensional embedding. **Step 8 — FC2 (256 → 10)** Output: **10** logits → softmax → 10 class probabilities. ### Parameter Count Audit $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{conv1}} &= 3^2 \cdot 1 \cdot 32 + 32 = 9 \cdot 32 + 32 = 320 \\ P_{\text{conv2}} &= 3^2 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 18{,}432 + 64 = 18{,}496 \\ P_{\text{conv3}} &= 3^2 \cdot 64 \cdot 128 + 128 = 73{,}728 + 128 = 73{,}856 \\ P_{\text{FC1}} &= 6{,}272 \cdot 256 + 256 = 1{,}605{,}632 + 256 = 1{,}605{,}888 \\ P_{\text{FC2}} &= 256 \cdot 10 + 10 = 2{,}570 \\ P_{\text{total}} &= 320 + 18{,}496 + 73{,}856 + 1{,}605{,}888 + 2{,}570 = \mathbf{1{,}701{,}130} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is *tiny* — about **1.7 million parameters**. VGG-16 has ~138M, GoogLeNet ~6.8M. This 80–800× difference in capacity is a *key* discussion point in the experiment. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pre-trained Model Adaptation | Aspect | Basic CNN | VGG-16 (Transfer) | GoogLeNet (Transfer) | |---|---|---|---| | Input Requirement | 1 × 28 × 28 | 3 × 224 × 224 (resize + replicate) | 3 × 224 × 224 (resize + replicate) | | Preprocessing | `transforms.ToTensor()` | `Resize(224) → Grayscale(3) → Normalize` | `Resize(224) → Grayscale(3) → Normalize` | | Backbone | Trained from scratch | ImageNet weights (`pretrained=True`) | ImageNet weights (`pretrained=True`) | | Modified Head | FC(256, 10) | Replace `classifier[6]` with `Linear(4096, 10)` | Replace `fc` with `Linear(1024, 10)` | | Trainable Params | ~1.7M | ~119M (feature extract) / 138M (fine-tune) | ~5M (feature extract) / 6.8M (fine-tune) | | Expected MNIST Acc. | **99.0–99.5%** | **99.3–99.7%** | **99.3–99.7%** | | Training Time (GPU) | ~2 min | ~8–12 min | ~5–8 min | | Overfitting Risk | Low | **High** (huge model, small data) | Moderate | | KTU Insight | Baseline benchmark | Demonstrates depth+transfer | Demonstrates efficiency+transfer | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to set `requires_grad=False` on frozen layers during feature-extraction. This causes PyTorch to update all 138M parameters — GPU memory explodes and training collapses. ## 3.3 Performance Change Equation The performance delta (the core "performance change" the KTU experiment asks for) is: $$\Delta_{\text{perf}} = \text{Acc}_{\text{pretrained}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{baseline}}$$ A typical measured outcome might be: $$ \Delta_{\text{VGG}} = 99.65\% - 99.20\% = +0.45\% $$ $$ \Delta_{\text{GoogLeNet}} = 99.55\% - 99.20\% = +0.35\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The accuracy gain on MNIST is *marginal* because MNIST is already "solved." The real value of pre-trained networks manifests on **harder datasets** (CIFAR-10, medical images, satellite imagery). This nuanced conclusion is what KTU examiners reward in viva. --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Experiment Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Load MNIST Dataset
60000 train + 10000 test"] B["Preprocess Images
Normalize and Convert to Tensors"] C{"Choose Model Architecture"} D["Basic CNN
3 Conv Layers + 2 FC Layers"] E["VGG-16 Transfer
Freeze Backbone Retrain Classifier"] F["GoogLeNet Transfer
Freeze Inception Retrain FC"] G["Define Loss and Optimizer
CrossEntropyLoss and Adam"] H["Training Loop
Forward Pass Backward Pass Update"] I["Validation
Compute Accuracy on Test Set"] J["Performance Comparison
Plot Confusion Matrix and Accuracy Curves"] K["Result Analysis
Baseline vs VGG vs GoogLeNet"] A --> B B --> C C --> D C --> E C --> F D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K ``` ## 4.2 Basic CNN Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Input
1x28x28"] C1["Conv2d
1 to 32 ch
3x3 kernel"] R1["ReLU"] C2["Conv2d
32 to 64 ch
3x3 kernel"] R2["ReLU"] P1["MaxPool2d
2x2 stride 2"] C3["Conv2d
64 to 128 ch
3x3 kernel"] R3["ReLU"] P2["MaxPool2d
2x2 stride 2"] FL["Flatten
6272 vector"] FC1["Linear
6272 to 256"] DR["Dropout
0.5"] FC2["Linear
256 to 10"] SM["Softmax
10 probabilities"] IN --> C1 --> R1 --> C2 --> R2 --> P1 --> C3 --> R3 --> P2 --> FL --> FC1 --> DR --> FC2 --> SM ``` ## 4.3 VGG-16 Transfer Learning Adaptation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FrozenBackbone["VGG-16 Backbone - FROZEN - ImageNet Weights"] V1["Block 1
2x Conv64 + Pool"] V2["Block 2
2x Conv128 + Pool"] V3["Block 3
3x Conv256 + Pool"] V4["Block 4
3x Conv512 + Pool"] V5["Block 5
3x Conv512 + Pool"] V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 end subgraph TrainableHead["NEW Classification Head - TRAINABLE"] F1["Flatten
25088 vector"] F2["Linear 4096 + ReLU + Dropout"] F3["Linear 4096 + ReLU + Dropout"] F4["Linear 10 - Softmax"] F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 end V5 --> F1 ``` ## 4.4 GoogLeNet Inception Module Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR IN2["Input Feature Map"] subgraph InceptionModule["Inception Module - 4 Parallel Branches"] B1["1x1 Conv"] B2["1x1 Conv then 3x3 Conv"] B3["1x1 Conv then 5x5 Conv"] B4["3x3 MaxPool then 1x1 Conv"] end CAT["Depth Concatenation
Along Channel Axis"] OUT2["Output Feature Map
Multi-scale Features Fused"] IN2 --> B1 IN2 --> B2 IN2 --> B3 IN2 --> B4 B1 --> CAT B2 --> CAT B3 --> CAT B4 --> CAT CAT --> OUT2 ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Performance Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Experiment Run"] A --> B["Train Basic CNN"] A --> C["Train VGG16 Transfer"] A --> D["Train GoogLeNet Transfer"] B --> E["Accuracy 99.20 percent"] C --> F["Accuracy 99.65 percent"] D --> G["Accuracy 99.55 percent"] E --> H["Tabulate and Plot"] F --> H G --> H H --> I["Conclusion
Transfer Learning Marginal Gain on MNIST"] ``` --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** **Define the MNIST dataset and list its key statistical properties used in deep learning experiments.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The MNIST (Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology) dataset is a benchmark collection of handwritten digits used for image classification tasks. Key properties: - **Total samples:** 70,000 images (60,000 training + 10,000 testing) - **Image dimensions:** 28×28 pixels, grayscale (1 channel) - **Classes:** 10 (digits 0 through 9) - **Pixel range:** 0–255, normalized to [0, 1] before training - **Storage:** Accessible via `torchvision.datasets.MNIST` in PyTorch. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** **What is the role of the softmax function in a digit classification CNN?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The softmax function converts the raw logits from the final fully-connected layer into a normalized probability distribution over the 10 digit classes: $$P(y=c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{10} e^{z_j}}$$ It ensures all outputs sum to 1 and are non-negative, allowing interpretation as class probabilities. The predicted class is $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(y=c \mid \mathbf{x})$. It is paired with **cross-entropy loss** for gradient-based training. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer ONE full question (with both sub-parts) out of internal choices provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern, Model Q]** **(a)** Design a Convolutional Neural Network architecture for MNIST digit classification. Draw the layer-wise block diagram and compute the output feature map dimensions after each layer. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Architecture Design (4 marks):** | Layer | Type | Input Dim | Output Dim | Kernel / Params | |---|---|---|---|---| | L1 | Conv2d + ReLU | 1×28×28 | 32×28×28 | 3×3, pad=1 | | L2 | Conv2d + ReLU | 32×28×28 | 64×28×28 | 3×3, pad=1 | | L3 | MaxPool2d | 64×28×28 | 64×14×14 | 2×2, stride=2 | | L4 | Conv2d + ReLU | 64×14×14 | 128×14×14 | 3×3, pad=1 | | L5 | MaxPool2d | 128×14×14 | 128×7×7 | 2×2, stride=2 | | L6 | Flatten | 128×7×7 | 6272 | — | | L7 | Linear + ReLU + Dropout | 6272 | 256 | 0.5 dropout | | L8 | Linear | 256 | 10 | softmax output | **Dimensional Computation (3 marks):** Using $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(W_{\text{in}} + 2p - k)/s\rfloor + 1$: - **L1:** $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 2 - 3)/1\rfloor + 1 = 28$ → **32×28×28** - **L3:** $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(28 + 0 - 2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 14$ → **64×14×14** - **L5:** $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(14 + 0 - 2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 7$ → **128×7×7** - **L6:** Flatten: $128 \times 7 \times 7 = 6{,}272$ --- **(b)** Explain the **transfer learning** strategy for adapting VGG-16 to MNIST classification. List the preprocessing steps required. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Concept (3 marks):** Transfer learning leverages knowledge from a pre-trained model (VGG-16 trained on ImageNet with 1.4M images, 1000 classes) to solve a new task (MNIST, 10 classes) with limited data. Two strategies: - **Feature Extraction:** Freeze convolutional backbone, retrain only the new FC classifier head. - **Fine-Tuning:** Unfreeze last few conv blocks and train with small learning rate ($\eta = 10^{-4}$). **Preprocessing Steps (2 marks):** 1. **Resize:** `transforms.Resize((224, 224))` — VGG-16 expects 224×224 input. 2. **Channel Replication:** `transforms.Grayscale(3)` — replicate the 1-channel image to 3 channels (RGB). 3. **Normalization:** Use ImageNet means `[0.485, 0.456, 0.406]` and stds `[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]`. 4. **Tensor Conversion:** `transforms.ToTensor()`. **Code Skeleton (2 marks):** ```python import torchvision.models as models model = models.vgg16(pretrained=True) for param in model.features.parameters(): param.requires_grad = False # Freeze backbone model.classifier[6] = nn.Linear(4096, 10) # New head ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **GoogLeNet (Inception-v1)** network. What is the advantage of the Inception module over a plain convolutional layer? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **GoogLeNet Architecture (4 marks):** - 22 layers deep (with auxiliary classifiers). - Core building block: **Inception module** (see SECTION 4.4). - Auxiliary classifiers at intermediate layers help with gradient flow during training. - Final layer: Global Average Pooling → FC (1000 classes for ImageNet) → Softmax. - ~6.8M parameters (≈ 20× fewer than VGG-16). **Inception Module (2 marks):** Performs convolutions of multiple filter sizes (1×1, 3×3, 5×5) plus a pooling branch **in parallel** on the same input, then concatenates their outputs along the channel axis. This captures **multi-scale spatial information** simultaneously. **Advantage (1 mark):** Parameter efficiency via **1×1 bottleneck convolutions** that reduce channel depth before expensive 3×3 and 5×5 ops — fewer parameters, less computation, better accuracy. --- **(b)** An experiment trains a **basic CNN** and a **VGG-16 (transfer)** model on MNIST. The basic CNN achieves 99.20% test accuracy in 2 minutes, while VGG-16 achieves 99.65% in 12 minutes. Analyze this performance change and discuss whether transfer learning is justified for MNIST. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Quantitative Analysis (3 marks):** $$\Delta_{\text{acc}} = 99.65\% - 99.20\% = +0.45\%$$ $$\Delta_{\text{time}} = 12 - 2 = +10 \text{ minutes (500% increase)}$$ $$\text{Accuracy-per-minute ratio} = \frac{99.65}{12} = 8.30 \quad \text{vs} \quad \frac{99.20}{2} = 49.60$$ **Qualitative Discussion (3 marks):** | Criterion | Basic CNN | VGG-16 Transfer | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Accuracy | 99.20% | 99.65% | Marginal gain | | Training Time | 2 min | 12 min | 6× slower | | GPU Memory | Low | High | Resource-heavy | | Inference Time | Fast | Slower | Latency penalty | | Generalization | Good | Risk of overfit | Comparable | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Transfer learning with VGG-16 is **not justified** for MNIST alone — the +0.45% gain does not justify the 6× training cost. However, the experiment's pedagogical value lies in **demonstrating the transfer-learning workflow**, which is critical for harder datasets (CIFAR-10, medical imaging, satellite imagery) where gains can be 5–15%. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - ❌ **Do not** claim VGG/GoogLeNet "always" beat basic CNN — the answer depends on dataset complexity. > - ❌ **Do not** forget to **resize MNIST to 224×224** and **replicate channels** — a classic 2-mark deduction. > - ❌ **Do not** train all VGG-16 parameters on 60k MNIST images without freezing — GPU OOM crash is a common viva failure. > - ✅ **Do** discuss *when* transfer learning is justified (small data, complex domain shift). > - ✅ **Do** mention the ImageNet normalization constants — examiners love this detail. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Pin this for the night before your lab exam!** ### ✅ Core Concepts - **MNIST** = 70,000 grayscale 28×28 digits, 10 classes, normalized to [0,1]. - **CNN forward pass** = Conv → ReLU → Pool → ... → Flatten → FC → Softmax. - **Output dimension formula** = $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor(W_{\text{in}} + 2p - k)/s\rfloor + 1$. ### ✅ Architectures - **Basic CNN for MNIST:** 3 conv layers (32→64→128) + 2 FC layers (~1.7M params). - **VGG-16:** 16 weight layers, 3×3 kernels, ~138M params, expects 224×224 RGB input. - **GoogLeNet:** 22 layers, Inception modules with parallel 1×1/3×3/5×5 convs, ~6.8M params. ### ✅ Transfer Learning Essentials - **Freeze backbone:** `for param in model.features.parameters(): param.requires_grad = False`. - **Replace head:** Modify final FC layer to output 10 classes. - **Preprocessing:** Resize to 224×224, replicate grayscale to 3 channels, ImageNet normalization. - **Optimizer:** Adam with learning rate $10^{-3}$ (feature extraction) or $10^{-4}$ (fine-tuning). - **Loss:** `nn.CrossEntropyLoss()` — combines softmax + negative log-likelihood. ### ✅ Performance Comparison Metrics - **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Confusion Matrix.** - Expected accuracies: Basic CNN ≈ 99.0–99.5%, VGG-16/GoogLeNet ≈ 99.3–99.7%. - **Key insight:** MNIST is "saturated" — transfer learning benefits are marginal here but valuable as a learning exercise. ### ✅ Common PyTorch Snippets to Memorize ```python # Data Loading train_loader = DataLoader(MNIST(...), batch_size=64, shuffle=True) # Model Definition (Basic CNN) class Net(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 32, 3, padding=1) # ... (rest of layers) # VGG-16 Transfer model = models.vgg16(pretrained=True) for p in model.features.parameters(): p.requires_grad = False model.classifier[6] = nn.Linear(4096, 10) # Training Step optimizer.zero_grad() loss = criterion(model(x), y) loss.backward() optimizer.step() ``` ### ✅ KTU 2024 Viva Favorites 1. Why softmax and not sigmoid for multi-class? → Softmax normalizes to a probability distribution summing to 1. 2. Why does GoogLeNet use 1×1 convolutions? → Channel-wise dimensionality reduction (bottleneck). 3. Why freeze pre-trained layers? → Preserve learned features, reduce trainable params, prevent overfitting on small datasets. 4. What happens if you don't normalize inputs? → Slow/unstable convergence, vanishing/exploding gradients. 5. Difference between feature extraction and fine-tuning? → Feature extraction freezes all backbone weights; fine-tuning unfreezes some for task-specific adaptation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** Always tabulate your results (Basic CNN vs VGG-16 vs GoogLeNet) with columns for **Accuracy, Training Time, Parameters, and Inference Latency**. Examiners award full marks when the comparison is structured and quantitative. ---

topics.title: Implement a simple RNN for review classification using IMDB dataset and analyze the performance change while using LSTM and GRU instead of simple RNN
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL607 - DEEP LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement a simple RNN for review classification using IMDB dataset and analyze the performance change while using LSTM and GRU instead of simple RNN

# 🧠 Experiment 2: Recurrent Architectures (RNN, LSTM, GRU) for IMDB Sentiment Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Course Outcome Mapping**
> This experiment directly maps to **CO2 (Apply)**, **CO3 (Analyze)**, and **CO4 (Evaluate)** of PCADL607. Students are evaluated on the basis of: (1) correct architectural implementation, (2) preprocessing correctness, (3) performance comparison table, and (4) written justification for the observed performance shift from Simple RNN → LSTM → GRU.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)?

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data — such as text, time series, genomes, handwriting, or spoken words. Unlike a feedforward network, an RNN maintains an internal **hidden state** $h_t$ that acts as a form of memory, allowing it to use information from previous time steps to influence the current output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An RNN is a neural network with cyclic (recurrent) connections whose hidden state is a function of both the current input and the hidden state of the previous time step. Mathematically, the hidden state update at time $t$ is governed by $h_t = f_W(h_{t-1}, x_t)$, where $f_W$ is a parameterized non-linear function shared across all time steps.

### 1.2 What are LSTM and GRU?

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks are a specialized variant of RNNs introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**. They are explicitly designed to avoid the **vanishing gradient problem** through a gated memory cell architecture.

**Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** networks, introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**, are a simplified variant of LSTMs that combine the forget and input gates into a single **update gate**, making them computationally cheaper while often delivering comparable accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Distinction (Board-Favourite Point):**
> - **Simple RNN**: 1 hidden state, 1 tanh activation → suffers from short-term memory.
> - **LSTM**: 2 states (cell state $C_t$ + hidden state $h_t$), **3 gates** (forget, input, output).
> - **GRU**: 1 hidden state, **2 gates** (reset, update). No separate cell state.

### 1.3 The IMDB Dataset

The **IMDB Movie Review Sentiment Dataset** is a benchmark binary classification corpus consisting of **50,000** highly polar movie reviews (split 50/50 into training and testing sets of 25,000 each) from the Internet Movie Database. Each review is labeled as either **positive (1)** or **negative (0)**. The vocabulary is restricted to the **top 10,000 most frequent words**, and each review is encoded as a sequence of word indices.

### 1.4 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Restaurant Review"

Imagine you are reading a long, complex movie review aloud to a friend. As you read each word, you build a **mental summary** of what has been said so far:

- A **Simple RNN** is like someone with a **tiny notepad** who can only remember the last few words. By the time the reviewer writes *"…but the ending was so predictable it ruined the otherwise brilliant plot"*, the RNN has already **forgotten** that the plot was originally praised. Its memory is too short.
- An **LSTM** is like a careful reader with a **detailed notebook and three sticky notes** — a *Forget Note* (to erase irrelevant info), an *Input Note* (to write down new important info), and an *Output Note* (to decide what to verbalize). The LSTM can remember *"brilliant plot"* all the way to the end of the sentence and correctly classify it as **positive**.
- A **GRU** is like an LSTM with a **simpler, more efficient notebook** — it uses only *Update* and *Reset* notes. It is faster to write in, and for many sentences, it captures the same idea with less overhead.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for the experiment?** Sentiment classification requires the network to integrate context across long word sequences. We will empirically demonstrate that Simple RNN underperforms because of vanishing gradients, while LSTM and GRU dramatically improve accuracy.

### 1.5 Standard Evaluation Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | — | Fraction of correct predictions |
| **Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** | $\mathcal{L}$ | Penalizes wrong confident predictions |
| **Validation Loss** | $val\_loss$ | Tracks overfitting |
| **Training Time / Epoch** | $s$ | Efficiency comparison |
| **Parameters** | $P$ | Model size |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Simple RNN — The Mathematical Engine

A Simple RNN unrolls across $T$ time steps. At each time step $t$:

- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ : input vector (word embedding of dimension $d$)
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ : hidden state vector of dimension $H$
- $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times H}$ : input-to-hidden weight matrix
- $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times H}$ : hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight matrix
- $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ : hidden bias vector

**Forward pass at time step $t$:**

$$z_t = W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

$$h_t = \tanh(z_t)$$

**Final output for binary classification (sigmoid head):**

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(W_{hy} \, h_T + b_y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Simple RNN fails on long reviews:** During **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, gradients are multiplied $T$ times by the same Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$. If the spectral radius of $W_{hh}$ is $< 1$, the gradient shrinks **exponentially** to zero — this is the **vanishing gradient problem**. IMDB reviews have an average length of **~234 words** after padding, far beyond the effective memory of a Simple RNN.

### 2.2 LSTM — The Gated Memory Cell

The LSTM introduces a **cell state** $C_t$ (the long-term memory highway) and three learnable gates:

- **Forget Gate** $f_t$ : decides what to discard from $C_{t-1}$
- **Input Gate** $i_t$ : decides which new values to write to $C_t$
- **Output Gate** $o_t$ : decides what to expose as the next hidden state

**LSTM Forward Pass Equations:**

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Hadamard product $\odot$ is **element-wise multiplication**. The cell state $C_t$ is updated via **additive** interactions (the $+$ operation), which provides a near-constant error carousel — this is precisely why gradients do not vanish across long sequences.

### 2.3 GRU — The Streamlined Variant

The GRU merges the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$ and combines cell state and hidden state:

**GRU Forward Pass Equations:**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \quad \text{(Update Gate)}$$

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) \quad \text{(Reset Gate)}$$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

### 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Architecture | Core Recurrence | Gates | States | Parameter Count (per cell) | Gradient Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple RNN** | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}x_t + W_{hh}h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | 0 | 1 ($h_t$) | $dH + H^2 + H$ | Multiplicative → **vanishes** |
| **LSTM** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | 3 ($f, i, o$) | 2 ($C_t, h_t$) | $4(dH + H^2 + H)$ | Additive → **stable** |
| **GRU** | $h_t = (1-z_t)h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ | 2 ($z, r$) | 1 ($h_t$) | $3(dH + H^2 + H)$ | Additive → **stable** |

| Loss Function | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} [y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)]$ | IMDB (2 classes) |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification metric |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **E-Commerce** | Amazon / Flipkart product review sentiment (decision support) |
| **Finance** | Earnings call transcript sentiment → stock movement prediction |
| **Healthcare** | Patient discharge note classification |
| **Social Media Analytics** | Twitter / Reddit moderation pipelines |
| **Customer Support** | Auto-tagging of tickets as urgent / non-urgent |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Why LSTM Solves Vanishing Gradients

Consider the gradient of loss $\mathcal{L}$ at time $T$ with respect to hidden state $h_t$ where $t < T$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$$

**For Simple RNN** with $\tanh$ activation (derivative $\le 1$) and recurrent weight $W_{hh}$:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(z_k)) \cdot W_{hh}$$

The product of $T-t$ such Jacobians gives:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \right\|^T \le (\lambda_{\max} W_{hh})^T \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda_{\max} < 1 \implies \text{exponential decay}$$

**For LSTM**, the gradient w.r.t. cell state $C_t$ has the form:

$$\frac{\partial C_k}{\partial C_{k-1}} = \text{diag}(f_k) \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

Since each $f_k \in [0,1]$ is a **learned** scalar, the network can keep $f_k \approx 1$ for the relevant dimensions, allowing:

$$\prod_{k=t+1}^{T} f_k \approx 1 \quad \text{when forget gates are open}$$

This is the **constant error carousel** — gradients flow over long distances without attenuation.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If asked *"Why does LSTM outperform Simple RNN on long sequences?"*, this product form and the forget-gate mechanism is the expected answer. KTU examiners award full marks (7/7) for explicitly stating the additive cell-state update and the learnable forget gate.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Performance Expectations (SOTA Reference)

Empirically reported results on IMDB (Keras default config, maxlen=200, vocab=10,000):

| Model | Trainable Params | Test Accuracy (approx.) | Test Loss | Train Time/Epoch (GPU) | Convergence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple RNN** | ~12k–66k | **80% – 83%** | 0.45 – 0.55 | ~10 s | Slow, plateaus early |
| **LSTM** | ~80k–170k | **86% – 88%** | 0.30 – 0.35 | ~40 s | Slower per step, higher final accuracy |
| **GRU** | ~60k–130k | **86% – 88%** | 0.30 – 0.35 | ~30 s | Slightly faster than LSTM, similar accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> Exact numbers depend on hyperparameters (embedding dim, hidden units, dropout, batch size). KTU lab evaluation expects the student to **record their own observed values** and provide a written comparison — the table above is the *qualitative* reference.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Code Implementation (Python / TensorFlow-Keras)

The following is the **complete, executable** lab code covering all three models. This is the typical expected submission for PCADL607.

```python
# ============================================================
# Experiment 2: RNN vs LSTM vs GRU on IMDB Sentiment Dataset
# Course: PCADL607 - Deep Learning Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
# ============================================================

import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import imdb
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing import sequence
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, SimpleRNN, LSTM, GRU, Dense, Dropout
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import EarlyStopping
import time
import pandas as pd

# ---------- 1. Load and preprocess ----------
print("[INFO] Loading IMDB dataset (top 10,000 words)...")
(X_train, y_train), (X_test, y_test) = imdb.load_data(num_words=10000)

maxlen = 200
print(f"[INFO] Padding sequences to maxlen = {maxlen}")
X_train = sequence.pad_sequences(X_train, maxlen=maxlen)
X_test  = sequence.pad_sequences(X_test,  maxlen=maxlen)

print(f"[INFO] X_train shape: {X_train.shape}, y_train shape: {y_train.shape}")
print(f"[INFO] X_test  shape: {X_test.shape},  y_test  shape: {y_test.shape}")

# ---------- 2. Hyperparameters ----------
vocab_size  = 10000
embed_dim   = 64
hidden_units = 64
batch_size  = 64
epochs      = 8
patience    = 2

# ---------- 3. Model builder ----------
def build_model(cell_type="RNN"):
    model = Sequential(name=f"IMDB_{cell_type}")
    model.add(Embedding(input_dim=vocab_size,
                        output_dim=embed_dim,
                        input_length=maxlen))
    if cell_type == "RNN":
        model.add(SimpleRNN(hidden_units, activation="tanh", return_sequences=False))
    elif cell_type == "LSTM":
        model.add(LSTM(hidden_units, dropout=0.2, recurrent_dropout=0.2))
    elif cell_type == "GRU":
        model.add(GRU(hidden_units, dropout=0.2, recurrent_dropout=0.2))
    model.add(Dropout(0.3))
    model.add(Dense(1, activation="sigmoid"))
    model.compile(optimizer="adam",
                  loss="binary_crossentropy",
                  metrics=["accuracy"])
    return model

# ---------- 4. Train + evaluate all three ----------
results = []
histories = {}
early_stop = EarlyStopping(monitor="val_loss", patience=patience, restore_best_weights=True)

for cell in ["RNN", "LSTM", "GRU"]:
    print(f"\n{'='*60}\n[INFO] Training Simple {cell} model...\n{'='*60}")
    model = build_model(cell)
    model.summary()
    t0 = time.time()
    h = model.fit(X_train, y_train,
                  validation_split=0.2,
                  batch_size=batch_size,
                  epochs=epochs,
                  callbacks=[early_stop],
                  verbose=1)
    train_time = time.time() - t0
    test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test, verbose=0)
    results.append({"Model": cell,
                    "Test Accuracy": round(test_acc, 4),
                    "Test Loss": round(test_loss, 4),
                    "Training Time (s)": round(train_time, 2),
                    "Parameters": model.count_params()})
    histories[cell] = h.history

# ---------- 5. Comparison Table ----------
df = pd.DataFrame(results)
print("\n========== FINAL COMPARISON ==========")
print(df.to_string(index=False))
df.to_csv("rnn_lstm_gru_comparison.csv", index=False)
```

### 3.4 Expected Console Output Snapshot

```text
[INFO] Loading IMDB dataset (top 10,000 words)...
[INFO] Padding sequences to maxlen = 200
[INFO] X_train shape: (25000, 200), y_train shape: (25000,)

========== FINAL COMPARISON ==========
Model  Test Accuracy  Test Loss  Training Time (s)  Parameters
  RNN         0.8123     0.4891             87.42         12865
 LSTM         0.8745     0.3012            312.18        82345
  GRU         0.8712     0.3154            256.74        61945
```

### 3.5 Lab Record Observations to Write Down

1. Simple RNN plateaus quickly — val loss stops decreasing after 2–3 epochs.
2. LSTM achieves the **highest test accuracy** but is the **slowest** to train.
3. GRU achieves accuracy **comparable to LSTM** with **fewer parameters** and **faster training** — the sweet spot for deployment.
4. Increasing `maxlen` improves LSTM/GRU but barely helps Simple RNN (proves long-term memory limit).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Simple RNN Cell Unrolled

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef hiddenStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000

    xt1["x t-1"]:::inputStyle
    xt2["x t"]:::inputStyle
    xt3["x t+1"]:::inputStyle
    ht1["h t-1"]:::hiddenStyle
    ht2["h t"]:::hiddenStyle
    ht3["h t+1"]:::hiddenStyle
    yt["y hat sigmoid"]:::outputStyle

    xt1 --> ht1
    ht1 --> ht2
    xt2 --> ht2
    ht2 --> ht3
    xt3 --> ht3
    ht3 --> yt

    ht1 -.recurrent weight W hh.-> ht2
    ht2 -.recurrent weight W hh.-> ht3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: LSTM Cell Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef gateStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef stateStyle fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef actStyle fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000

    xt["x t"] --> concat1["concat h t-1 , x t"]
    ht1["h t-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> ft["sigmoid FORGET GATE f t"]:::gateStyle
    concat1 --> it["sigmoid INPUT GATE i t"]:::gateStyle
    concat1 --> cct["tanh CANDIDATE C tilde t"]:::actStyle
    concat1 --> ot["sigmoid OUTPUT GATE o t"]:::gateStyle

    Ct1["C t-1"]:::stateStyle --> mul1["element wise multiply"]
    ft --> mul1
    mul1 --> add1["element wise add"]
    it --> add1
    cct --> add1
    add1 --> Ct["C t"]:::stateStyle

    Ct --> tanh1["tanh"]
    tanh1 --> mul2["element wise multiply"]
    ot --> mul2
    mul2 --> ht["h t"]:::stateStyle
    Ct -.carries long term memory.-> mul1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: GRU Cell Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef gateStyle fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef actStyle fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef stateStyle fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000

    xt["x t"] --> concat1["concat h t-1 , x t"]
    ht1["h t-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> zt["sigmoid UPDATE GATE z t"]:::gateStyle
    concat1 --> rt["sigmoid RESET GATE r t"]:::gateStyle

    ht1 --> mul1["element wise multiply"]
    rt --> mul1
    mul1 --> concat2["concat r t dot h t-1 , x t"]
    xt --> concat2
    concat2 --> hct["tanh CANDIDATE h tilde t"]:::actStyle

    zt --> mul2["element wise multiply with h tilde t"]
    hct --> mul2
    zt --> mul3["1 minus z t element wise multiply with h t-1"]
    ht1 --> mul3
    mul3 --> add1["element wise add"]
    mul2 --> add1
    add1 --> ht["h t"]:::stateStyle
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Full Experiment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef stepStyle fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef modelStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    classDef evalStyle fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    A["Step 1: Load IMDB dataset vocab 10000"]:::stepStyle
    B["Step 2: Pad sequences to maxlen 200"]:::stepStyle
    C["Step 3: Train Test split 80 20"]:::stepStyle
    D["Step 4a: Build Simple RNN model"]:::modelStyle
    E["Step 4b: Build LSTM model"]:::modelStyle
    F["Step 4c: Build GRU model"]:::modelStyle
    G["Step 5: Train each model with EarlyStopping"]:::stepStyle
    H["Step 6: Evaluate on X test y test"]:::evalStyle
    I["Step 7: Tabulate accuracy loss time params"]:::evalStyle
    J["Step 8: Plot training curves and write inference"]:::evalStyle

    A --> B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H --> I --> J
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> *What is the vanishing gradient problem in a Simple RNN? Why does it occur?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The vanishing gradient problem refers to the exponential decay of gradient magnitudes during backpropagation through time (BPTT) in a Simple RNN. It occurs because gradients are computed as repeated products of Jacobian matrices $\prod_{k} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ across $T$ time steps. When the spectral radius of the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ is less than 1, this product shrinks toward zero, preventing the network from learning long-range dependencies. In IMDB reviews (avg. 234 words), this causes the network to forget earlier sentiment cues, hurting classification accuracy. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Differentiate between the gating mechanisms of LSTM and GRU.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell state | Separate $C_t$ and $h_t$ | Single $h_t$ |
| Memory control | Forget gate explicitly erases $C_{t-1}$ | Update gate interpolates between old and new |
| Parameters | $4(dH + H^2 + H)$ | $3(dH + H^2 + H)$ |
| Accuracy on IMDB | Slightly higher | Comparable, faster |

(3 marks)

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*
>
> **(a)** Derive the forward pass equations of an LSTM cell and explain how its gating mechanism mitigates the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Implement a complete pipeline in Python (TensorFlow/Keras) to train and compare a **Simple RNN**, an **LSTM**, and a **GRU** model on the IMDB sentiment dataset. Tabulate the test accuracy, test loss, parameter count, and training time for all three models. Write a short inference (4–5 lines) on the observed performance change. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define the gates (2 marks):**
The LSTM introduces three sigmoid gates driven by concatenated input $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(forget gate)}$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(input gate)}$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(output gate)}$$

**Step 2 — Cell state candidate (1 mark):**

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Step 3 — Cell state update — the key equation (2 marks):**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 4 — Hidden state output (1 mark):**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Step 5 — Why vanishing gradient is mitigated (1 mark):**
The gradient $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ contains **only** the forget gate (no $\tanh$ derivative, no weight matrix multiplication). The network learns to keep $f_t \approx 1$ for relevant memory dimensions, creating a **constant error carousel** where $\prod_{k} f_k \approx 1$. This allows gradients to flow unchanged over hundreds of time steps — exactly what long IMDB reviews require.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Imports and data loading (1 mark):**
```python
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import imdb
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing import sequence
(X_train, y_train), (X_test, y_test) = imdb.load_data(num_words=10000)
X_train = sequence.pad_sequences(X_train, maxlen=200)
X_test  = sequence.pad_sequences(X_test,  maxlen=200)
```

**Step 2 — Model builder (2 marks):**
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, SimpleRNN, LSTM, GRU, Dense, Dropout

def build(cell):
    m = Sequential([Embedding(10000, 64, input_length=200)])
    if cell == "RNN":  m.add(SimpleRNN(64))
    if cell == "LSTM": m.add(LSTM(64, dropout=0.2, recurrent_dropout=0.2))
    if cell == "GRU":  m.add(GRU(64,  dropout=0.2, recurrent_dropout=0.2))
    m.add(Dropout(0.3)); m.add(Dense(1, activation="sigmoid"))
    m.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="binary_crossentropy", metrics=["accuracy"])
    return m
```

**Step 3 — Training loop and evaluation (2 marks):**
```python
import time, pandas as pd
results = []
for c in ["RNN", "LSTM", "GRU"]:
    model = build(c)
    t0 = time.time()
    model.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_split=0.2,
              batch_size=64, epochs=8, verbose=0)
    elapsed = time.time() - t0
    loss, acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test, verbose=0)
    results.append([c, round(acc,4), round(loss,4), round(elapsed,2), model.count_params()])
df = pd.DataFrame(results, columns=["Model","Test Acc","Test Loss","Time(s)","Params"])
print(df)
```

**Step 4 — Expected comparison table (1 mark):**

| Model | Test Acc | Test Loss | Time (s) | Params |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple RNN | 0.81 | 0.49 | ~90 | ~12,865 |
| LSTM | 0.87 | 0.30 | ~310 | ~82,345 |
| GRU | 0.87 | 0.31 | ~255 | ~61,945 |

**Step 5 — Written Inference (1 mark):**
*Simple RNN underperforms because its single tanh-based recurrence suffers from vanishing gradients and cannot retain long-range sentiment cues. LSTM overcomes this via its gated cell-state mechanism and achieves the highest accuracy. GRU matches LSTM closely in accuracy while using ~25% fewer parameters and training noticeably faster, making it the preferred choice for production deployment.*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a **GRU** with neat equations. Compare its parameter count with that of an LSTM cell. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **preprocessing pipeline** for the IMDB dataset (tokenization, vocabulary capping, padding) and justify why each step is necessary. What is the role of the **Embedding layer** and how does it differ from a one-hot encoding? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — GRU architecture overview (1 mark):**
The GRU consolidates the LSTM's forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$, and adds a **reset gate** $r_t$ to control how much past information to forget when computing the candidate activation.

**Step 2 — Update gate equation (1.5 marks):**
$$z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

**Step 3 — Reset gate equation (1.5 marks):**
$$r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**Step 4 — Candidate hidden state (1 mark):**
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

**Step 5 — Final hidden state — the interpolation (1 mark):**
$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Step 6 — Parameter comparison (1 mark):**
For embedding dim $d$ and hidden size $H$:
- LSTM: $4(dH + H^2 + H)$ — four weight matrices for $f, i, C, o$
- GRU: $3(dH + H^2 + H)$ — three weight matrices for $z, r, h$
- **Saving:** GRU uses **25% fewer parameters** for the same $d, H$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Tokenization (1.5 marks):**
IMDB reviews are raw English text. Keras' `imdb.load_data()` internally applies a tokenizer that maps each word to a unique integer index. This converts variable-length text into fixed-format integer sequences compatible with neural networks.

**Step 2 — Vocabulary capping (1.5 marks):**
Setting `num_words=10000` retains only the top 10,000 most frequent words. Rare words (occurring fewer than ~5 times) are mapped to a single **OOV (out-of-vocabulary)** token. This is necessary to bound the embedding matrix size, reduce noise from rare typos, and prevent overfitting.

**Step 3 — Padding (1.5 marks):**
Reviews have variable lengths (some 50 words, some 1500+). `sequence.pad_sequences(..., maxlen=200)` either pads shorter reviews with zeros (pre-padding) or truncates longer ones. This produces uniform tensor shape $(N, 200)$, required for batch processing in Keras.

**Step 4 — Embedding layer role (1.5 marks):**
The `Embedding(10000, 64)` layer maps each word index to a **learned dense vector of dimension 64**. It transforms the sparse integer input into a dense semantic representation. Unlike one-hot encoding (sparse, 10,000-dimensional, no semantic information), embeddings are **dense, low-dimensional, and trained end-to-end** — semantically similar words end up with similar vectors.

**Step 5 — Why this matters for RNNs (1 mark):**
A one-hot input of size 10,000 multiplied by a recurrent matrix would explode the parameter count and destroy semantic relationships. The embedding compresses this into 64 dimensions and provides the recurrent layer with semantically meaningful, real-valued input vectors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **No comparison table** → loses 2 marks. The tabulation of accuracy, loss, time, and parameters is **mandatory** in the record.
> 2. **Confusing cell state with hidden state** in LSTM → costs 1 mark. Always state $C_t$ (long-term) and $h_t$ (short-term/output) explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention BPTT** when explaining vanishing gradients → lose 1 mark. The phrase *"Backpropagation Through Time"* is a KTU keyword.
> 4. **Plotting only training accuracy** and not validation curves → lose 1 mark. Both curves are required to demonstrate overfitting analysis.
> 5. **No written inference** explaining the observed shift RNN → LSTM → GRU → lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Using ReLU inside Simple RNN** instead of `tanh` → loses 1 mark; default for `SimpleRNN` is `tanh`, and this is expected.

---

### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IMDB dataset**: 50,000 reviews, binary labels, top 10,000 words, average length ~234 words.
- **Simple RNN**: one hidden state, tanh activation, suffers from **vanishing gradient** during BPTT.
- **LSTM**: two states ($C_t$ and $h_t$), **three gates** (forget, input, output), solves vanishing gradient via **additive cell state** update.
- **GRU**: one hidden state, **two gates** (update, reset), **25% fewer parameters** than LSTM, often similar accuracy.
- **Preprocessing**: tokenize → cap vocab → pad to fixed length → embedding layer.
- **Embedding layer**: dense, learnable, low-dim representation (e.g., 64-d) replacing one-hot.
- **Loss**: Binary Cross-Entropy with sigmoid output (1 unit, sigmoid activation).
- **Optimizer**: Adam is the default; **EarlyStopping** is a must to prevent overfitting.
- **Performance order** on IMDB: **LSTM ≈ GRU > Simple RNN** (accuracy); **GRU > LSTM > Simple RNN** (training time).
- **GRU is the practical sweet spot**: comparable accuracy, fewer parameters, faster training.
- **Code keywords for KTU viva**: `pad_sequences`, `Embedding`, `return_sequences`, `recurrent_dropout`, `EarlyStopping`, `model.count_params()`.
- **Key paper references (no fabrication)**: Hochreiter & Schmidhuber 1997 (LSTM); Cho et al. 2014 (GRU).
- **Exam one-liner to memorize**: *"The forget gate of the LSTM creates a constant error carousel that prevents gradient decay across long sequences."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement time series forecasting prediction for NIFTY-50 dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL607 - DEEP LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement time series forecasting prediction for NIFTY-50 dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Series Forecasting** is a sequence-to-value (or sequence-to-sequence) supervised learning paradigm where a model learns a mapping from a finite history of temporally ordered observations $\lbrace x_{t-T+1}, x_{t-T+2}, \dots, x_{t} \rbrace$ to a future value (or values) $\hat{x}_{t+1}, \dots, \hat{x}_{t+H}$, where $T$ is the lookback window and $H$ is the forecast horizon.

The **NIFTY-50** is the flagship benchmark stock market index of the National Stock Exchange (NSE) of India, comprising **50** large-cap, liquid equities across 14 sectors. For Deep Learning applications, it is treated as a univariate (Closing Price) or multivariate (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume — **OHLCV**) sequential dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCADL607 – Module 2):** *Students are expected to implement, train, and evaluate at least one deep recurrent architecture (RNN/LSTM/GRU) on a real-world sequential dataset and justify the choice of hyper-parameters.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Lookback Window ($T$):** Number of past time steps used as input.
> - **Forecast Horizon ($H$):** Number of future steps to predict.
> - **Univariate Forecasting:** Only one variable (e.g., Close price).
> - **Multivariate Forecasting:** Multiple correlated features used simultaneously.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **predict tomorrow's temperature** by looking at the last 60 days of weather data. You don't just memorize — you detect *patterns*: a cold front, a warming trend, or a seasonal cycle. An **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** network does exactly this for stock prices.

**Real-world analogy — "The Indian Stock Market Storyteller":**
Think of the NIFTY-50 closing price as a story written day by day over the last 25 years. Each day's price is a *word* in that story. A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is like a reader who can remember the previous few paragraphs (short-term memory) but forgets older ones. An **LSTM** is a *seasoned analyst* with a notepad — it selectively writes down important events (gates), forgets noise, and uses that notepad to predict the next chapter. **GRU** is a faster, slightly simpler version of this analyst.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value for NIFTY-50 |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Lookback window | **60 trading days** (≈ 3 months) |
| $H$ | Forecast horizon | **1 to 30 days** |
| $x_t$ | Price at time $t$ | Daily close in **INR** |
| $r_t$ | Log-return | $\log(P_t / P_{t-1})$ |
| $\mu, \sigma$ | Mean, std deviation of returns | Used for **Z-score normalization** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NIFTY-50 is Hard to Forecast:** The Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) suggests prices follow a *random walk*, making $\hat{x}_{t+1} \approx x_t$ a surprisingly strong baseline. Deep learning adds value only when **non-linear temporal patterns** exist.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

A robust NIFTY-50 forecasting pipeline follows this structured logic:

1. **Data Acquisition**
   - Source: Yahoo Finance (`yfinance` API), NSE India portal, or Kaggle.
   - Ticker symbol: `^NSEI` for NIFTY-50 index.

2. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**
   - Plot raw Close prices.
   - Check stationarity via **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test**.
   - Identify trend and seasonality components.

3. **Preprocessing**
   - Handle missing values via forward-fill (`ffill`).
   - Apply **Min-Max scaling** to $[0, 1]$ OR **Z-score standardization**.
   - Convert to **supervised learning format** via sliding window.

4. **Sliding Window Construction**
   - For each index $i$, create input $X_i = \lbrace x_{i-T}, \dots, x_{i-1} \rbrace$ and target $y_i = x_i$.

5. **Train-Validation-Test Split**
   - Chronological split (NEVER shuffle time series!): 70% train / 15% val / 15% test.

6. **Model Architecture (LSTM/GRU)**
   - Input layer → LSTM/GRU layer(s) → Dense layer → Output.

7. **Training**
   - Loss: **MSE** or **Huber Loss**.
   - Optimizer: **Adam** (default lr = 0.001).
   - Regularization: Dropout, Early Stopping.

8. **Evaluation & Visualization**
   - Compute RMSE, MAE, MAPE.
   - Plot **Predicted vs. Actual** close prices.

### 2.2 LSTM Cell — Core Equations

An LSTM cell maintains two state vectors: **cell state** $C_t$ and **hidden state** $h_t$. Three gates control information flow.

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = sigmoid activation (gate output in $[0,1]$)
- $\tanh$ = hyperbolic tangent (candidate values in $[-1, 1]$)
- $\odot$ = element-wise (Hadamard) product
- $f_t$ = **forget gate**, $i_t$ = **input gate**, $o_t$ = **output gate**
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ = learnable weight matrices
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ = learnable bias vectors

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LSTM over Vanilla RNN?** Vanilla RNNs suffer from the **vanishing gradient problem** for sequences longer than ~20 steps. LSTMs use additive cell-state updates $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + \dots$, which preserve gradient flow over hundreds of steps — critical for capturing long-term NIFTY trends.

### 2.3 GRU Cell — Simplified Variant

GRU merges forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$ and uses a **reset gate** $r_t$:

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t])
$$

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t])
$$

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t])
$$

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

**KTU Quick Tip:** GRUs have **~25% fewer parameters** than LSTMs and train faster — often the preferred choice for short-window NIFTY forecasting.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU Exams**

| Concept | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Return** | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | Daily price change | Dimensionless |
| **Log Return** | $r_t = \log\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)$ | Better statistical properties | Dimensionless |
| **Min-Max Scaling** | $x'_t = \dfrac{x_t - \min(X)}{\max(X) - \min(X)}$ | Normalize to $[0,1]$ | Unitless |
| **Z-Score Scaling** | $x'_t = \dfrac{x_t - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal transform | Unitless |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Primary loss function | INR² |
| **Root MSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Interpretable in INR | INR |
| **Mean Absolute Error** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers | INR |
| **MAPE** | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100}{N}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right\vert$ | Percentage error | **%** |
| **Directional Accuracy** | $\text{DA} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \mathbb{1}[\text{sign}(y_t - y_{t-1}) = \text{sign}(\hat{y}_t - y_{t-1})]$ | Trading utility | **%** |
| **Huber Loss** | $L_\delta = \begin{cases} 0.5(y - \hat{y})^2 & \text{if } \vert y - \hat{y} \vert \le \delta \\ \delta \vert y - \hat{y} \vert - 0.5\delta^2 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Robust training | INR or INR² |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule for Tables:** When writing MAE, MAPE, or any absolute value, **never** use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside a markdown table row. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. KTU examiners **will not fix** broken tables in answer sheets.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading:** Hedge funds use LSTM/Transformer hybrids to predict intraday NIFTY movements.
- **Risk Management:** VaR (Value-at-Risk) estimation using forecasted volatility.
- **Portfolio Rebalancing:** Monthly rebalancing decisions driven by 30-day forecasts.
- **Macro-Economic Policy:** RBI uses similar models on broader indices for liquidity decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sliding Window Mathematics

Given a univariate price series $P = \lbrace P_0, P_1, P_2, \dots, P_{N-1} \rbrace$ of length $N$, we construct $(N - T)$ supervised learning samples with lookback $T$ and horizon $H = 1$.

**Step 1 — Index definition:**
For sample index $i$ where $0 \le i \le N - T - 1$, the input window ends at index $i + T - 1$ and the target is at index $i + T$.

$$
X_i = \begin{bmatrix} P_i \\ P_{i+1} \\ \vdots \\ P_{i+T-1} \end{bmatrix}_{T \times 1}, \quad y_i = P_{i+T}
$$

**Step 2 — Reshape for LSTM input:**
PyTorch/Keras LSTMs expect a 3D tensor of shape **(samples, time_steps, features)**.

$$
X_{\text{LSTM}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N - T) \times T \times 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Chronological split:**
Define split points based on the temporal order, not random shuffling.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{train}} &= \lfloor 0.70 \cdot (N - T) \rfloor \\
N_{\text{val}} &= \lfloor 0.15 \cdot (N - T) \rfloor \\
N_{\text{test}} &= (N - T) - N_{\text{train}} - N_{\text{val}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Critical leakage prevention:**
The scaler (`MinMaxScaler` or `StandardScaler`) **must be fit ONLY on the training set** to prevent data leakage. Applying it to the entire dataset before splitting is a **common KTU mistake** that examiners heavily penalize.

### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) for LSTM

The LSTM overcomes vanishing gradients through its **additive cell-state path**. We derive why.

**Step 1 — Gradient of cell state w.r.t. previous cell state:**

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial f_t} \cdot \frac{\partial f_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t-1}}{\partial C_{t-1}} + \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial i_t} \cdot \frac{\partial i_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t-1}}{\partial C_{t-1}} + f_t
$$

**Step 2 — The diagonal contains the forget-gate term $f_t$:**

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \bigg|_{\text{diag}} = f_t \in [0, 1]
$$

**Step 3 — Chain rule over $k$ time steps:**

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} f_{t-j+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If $f_t \approx 1$ (forget gate open), gradients flow **unimpeded** across long sequences.
- If $f_t \approx 0$ (forget gate closed), gradients are **locally suppressed** — preventing irrelevant past information from dominating learning.
- This is why LSTM succeeds on NIFTY data with **cyclic macro events** (budgets, elections) that span years.

### 3.3 Derivation: MAPE Bound and Pitfall

**MAPE** is asymmetric — it penalizes under-forecasts more than over-forecasts.

**Step 1 — Substitute $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:**

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert \frac{e_i}{y_i} \right\vert
$$

**Step 2 — Lower bound check:**
If $\hat{y}_i \to 0$ and $y_i > 0$, then $\text{MAPE} \to \infty$ (division by zero / explosion).

**Step 3 — Conclusion for KTU:**
For very low NIFTY values (e.g., pre-2000 era when index was ~1000), MAPE can be misleading. **Prefer RMSE + Directional Accuracy** for low-priced regimes. Use **sMAPE** (symmetric MAPE) as a robust alternative.

$$
\text{sMAPE} = \frac{100}{N} \sum \frac{2 \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert}{\vert y_i \vert + \vert \hat{y}_i \vert}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Comparison

| Architecture | Parameters | Training Speed | Long-Range Memory | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | Fewest | Fastest | **Poor** (≤ 20 steps) | Toy demos only |
| **LSTM** | Highest | Slowest | **Excellent** | NIFTY long-window |
| **GRU** | Moderate | Moderate | **Good** | Short-window NIFTY |
| **1D-CNN** | Few | Fast | Limited receptive field | Pattern detection |
| **Transformer** | Massive | Slow (GPU-heavy) | **Excellent** (attention) | Research-grade |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For PCADL607 lab exams, **LSTM is the default expected answer**. Transformer-based forecasting is bonus, and a 1D-CNN is acceptable as a baseline. Vanilla RNNs are considered outdated and may lose marks unless explicitly justified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Data Source: Yahoo Finance NSEI] --> A1[Load CSV / API Call]
    A1 --> A2[Handle Missing Values: ffill]
    A2 --> A3[EDA: Plot Close Price]
    A3 --> A4[Stationarity Test ADF]
    A4 --> A5[Chronological Train Val Test Split 70 15 15]
    A5 --> A6[Fit Scaler on Train Only]
    A6 --> A7[Sliding Window T=60 H=1]
    A7 --> A8[LSTM Model Build]
    A8 --> A9[Training: Adam + MSE + Early Stop]
    A9 --> A10[Inverse Transform Predictions]
    A10 --> A11[Compute RMSE MAE MAPE DA]
    A11 --> A12[Plot Predicted vs Actual]
    A12 --> A13[Forecast Next 30 Days Recursive]
```

### 4.2 LSTM Cell Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Xt] --> concat1[Concat h_t-1 and Xt]
    hprev[h_t-1] --> concat1
    concat1 --> fg[Forget Gate sigmoid]
    concat1 --> ig[Input Gate sigmoid]
    concat1 --> cg[Candidate tanh]
    concat1 --> og[Output Gate sigmoid]
    cprev[C_t-1] --> mul1[Multiply]
    fg --> mul1
    ig --> mul2[Multiply]
    cg --> mul2
    mul1 --> add[Add]
    mul2 --> add
    add --> cnew[C_t]
    add --> tanh1[tanh]
    og --> mul3[Multiply]
    tanh1 --> mul3
    mul3 --> hnew[h_t]
    cnew --> outprop[Cell state to next step]
    hnew --> outpred[Hidden state to next step and output]
```

### 4.3 Sliding Window Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0[Day 0] --> s1[Day 1] --> s2[Day 2] --> s3[Day 3] --> s4[Day 4] --> s5[Day 5] --> s6[Day 6] --> s7[Day 7] --> s8[Day 8] --> s9[Day 9]
    subgraph sample1[Sample 1: Lookback 3 Predict 1]
        d0[s0] --> d1[s1] --> d2[s2]
        d2 --> d3[s3 is target]
    end
    subgraph sample2[Sample 2: Slide by 1]
        d4[s1] --> d5[s2] --> d6[s3]
        d6 --> d7[s4 is target]
    end
    subgraph sample3[Sample 3: Slide by 1]
        d8[s2] --> d9[s3] --> d10[s4]
        d10 --> d11[s5 is target]
    end
```

### 4.4 Model Layer Stack (Typical KTU-Acceptable Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Input Layer: shape 60, 1] --> L1[LSTM 64 units return sequences True]
    L1 --> L2[Dropout 0.2]
    L2 --> L3[LSTM 32 units return sequences False]
    L3 --> L4[Dropout 0.2]
    L4 --> L5[Dense 16 ReLU]
    L5 --> L6[Dense 1 Linear]
    L6 --> L7[Output: Predicted Close Price]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Fallback for Complex Systems)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputBlock[Input Stage]
        raw[Raw OHLCV Data] --> clean[Cleaned Series]
    end
    subgraph FeatEng[Feature Engineering Stage]
        clean --> scale[Scaled Series 0 to 1]
        clean --> ret[Log Returns]
        scale --> win[Windowed Tensors]
    end
    subgraph ModelStage[Deep Model Stage]
        win --> lstm1[LSTM Layer 1: 64 units]
        lstm1 --> lstm2[LSTM Layer 2: 32 units]
        lstm2 --> dense[Dense Output: 1 neuron]
    end
    subgraph EvalStage[Evaluation Stage]
        dense --> inv[Inverse Transform]
        inv --> met[Metrics: RMSE MAE DA]
        met --> plot[Visualization]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, June 2024]`
**Differentiate between vanilla RNN and LSTM. Why is LSTM preferred for NIFTY-50 forecasting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Cell state $C_t$ + hidden state $h_t$ |
| Gates | None | Forget, Input, Output |
| Long-range deps | Suffers vanishing gradient (≤ 20 steps) | Preserves via additive $C_t$ updates (100s of steps) |
| Parameters | Fewer | ~4× more |

**Why preferred for NIFTY-50:** NIFTY closing prices exhibit long-range temporal dependencies (macro-economic cycles, multi-year bull/bear phases). LSTM's gating mechanism selectively retains these patterns while filtering daily noise. *(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for NIFTY-specific justification)*

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the three gates in an LSTM cell with their respective activation functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Forget Gate $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$** — Uses **sigmoid** to decide what fraction of the previous cell state to discard. Output in $[0, 1]$: 0 = forget completely, 1 = keep fully. *(1 mark)*

2. **Input Gate $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$** — Uses **sigmoid** to control what new information is written to the cell state, combined with a **tanh** candidate $\tilde{C}_t$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Output Gate $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$** — Uses **sigmoid** to filter the squashed (tanh) cell state, producing the next hidden state $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
